Learn what a REST API is, how it works, and why it’s the backbone of modern web applications — with simple, real-world examples.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Introduction
If you’ve started learning web development, you’ve probably come across the term REST API. Whether you’re building a MERN application, a mobile app, or integrating with third-party services, REST APIs are everywhere.
But what exactly is a REST API?
When I first encountered the term, I found definitions filled with technical jargon. It wasn’t until I connected it to everyday experiences that the concept finally made sense. In this article, I’ll explain REST APIs in a simple and practical way using a real-world example.

What Is an API?
API stands for Application Programming Interface.
An API acts as a bridge between two software applications, allowing them to communicate with each other. Instead of accessing a database or service directly, one application sends a request to an API, which processes the request and returns the appropriate response.
Think of a restaurant:

• You are the customer.

• The kitchen prepares the food.

• The waiter takes your order to the kitchen and brings your food back.
The waiter is the API. You don’t enter the kitchen to prepare your meal, and the chefs don’t come to your table to take your order. The waiter handles the communication between both sides.
Similarly, in software development, the API connects the client (such as a web or mobile application) with the server.
Client (Browser/Mobile App)
│
▼
REST API
│
▼
Server + Database
What Is REST?
REST stands for Representational State Transfer.
Although the name may sound complicated, REST is simply a set of architectural principles for designing web APIs. REST APIs communicate over HTTP and use standard methods such as GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE to perform different operations.
Following these standards makes APIs consistent, predictable, and easy to use.

Understanding REST APIs with a Real-World Example
Imagine you’re using an online food delivery application.
Step 1: Viewing the Menu
You open the application and want to see all available meals.
The application sends the following request:
GET /api/menuThe server responds with:
[
{
"id": 1,
"name": "Chicken Burger",
"price": 1200
},
{
"id": 2,
"name": "Pizza",
"price": 2500
}
]The GET method retrieves information without modifying any data.
Step 2: Placing an Order
You decide to order a Chicken Burger.
The application sends:
POST /api/orderswith the following data:
{
"food": "Chicken Burger",
"quantity": 1
}The server stores the order in the database and returns a confirmation.
The POST method creates new data.
Step 3: Updating the Order
You decide you actually want two burgers instead of one.
The application sends:
PUT /api/orders/101
{
"food": "Chicken Burger",
"quantity": 2
}The PUT method replaces the existing resource with the new data.
Step 4: Updating Only One Field
Now you only want to change the delivery address.
The application sends:
PATCH /api/orders/101
{
"address": "Colombo"
}Unlike PUT, the PATCH method updates only the specified field while leaving everything else unchanged.
Step 5: Cancelling the Order
You change your mind and decide to cancel the order.
The application sends:
DELETE /api/orders/101The server removes the order from the database.
The DELETE method removes data permanently.

HTTP Methods Explained
Method Purpose Real-World Example GET Retrieve data View the restaurant menu POST Create new data Place a new order PUT Replace existing data Update the complete order PATCH Update part of the data Change only the delivery address DELETE Remove data Cancel an order
These five HTTP methods form the foundation of most REST APIs.

How Does a REST API Work?
Let’s see what happens behind the scenes when you click a button in an application:

• The user performs an action, such as clicking Place Order.

• The frontend application sends an HTTP request.

• The REST API receives the request.

• The server processes the request.

• The database stores or retrieves the required information.

• The server sends a response.

• The frontend updates the user interface.
User

▼
Frontend
│
▼
REST API
│
▼
Server

Database
│
▲
ResponseThis process usually takes only a few milliseconds.

Why REST APIs Are So Popular
REST APIs have become the standard for web development because they offer several advantages:

• Easy to understand and implement

• Platform-independent communication

• Supports multiple clients such as web, mobile, and desktop applications

• Scalable for small and large applications

• Lightweight communication using JSON

• Easy integration with third-party services
These benefits make REST APIs suitable for everything from small personal projects to enterprise-level applications.

Where Are REST APIs Used?
REST APIs power many of the applications we use every day. Some common examples include:

• Online shopping platforms

• Banking applications

• Food delivery services

• Ride-sharing applications

• Social media platforms

• Hotel booking systems

• Healthcare applications

• Learning Management Systems (LMS)

• Weather applications
Whenever one application communicates with another, there is a high chance that a REST API is involved.

Common Mistakes Beginners Make
When learning REST APIs, beginners often make these mistakes:

• Using POST instead of GET for retrieving data

• Confusing PUT and PATCH

• Ignoring HTTP status codes

• Returning inconsistent API responses

• Not validating user input

• Exposing sensitive information through APIs

• Forgetting to handle errors properly
Avoiding these mistakes early will help you build secure and reliable applications.

Key Takeaways

• An API enables communication between different software applications.

• REST is a popular architectural style for designing APIs.

• REST APIs use HTTP methods such as GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE.

• JSON is the most commonly used format for exchanging data.

• REST APIs are used in almost every modern web application.

• Learning REST APIs is an essential skill for frontend, backend, and full-stack developers.

Final Thoughts
REST APIs are one of the most important concepts in modern web development. Whether you’re building a portfolio project, preparing for software engineering interviews, or developing production-ready applications, understanding how REST APIs work will make you a more confident and capable developer.
Instead of memorizing technical definitions, think of a REST API as a bridge between the client and the server. It receives requests, communicates with the server, and returns the appropriate response to the client. This simple communication model powers almost every modern web application we use today.
From online shopping and food delivery apps to banking systems and social media platforms, REST APIs enable different applications to exchange data efficiently and securely. Once you understand this concept, learning backend frameworks like Express.js, Spring Boot, or Django — and integrating them with frontend frameworks like React — becomes much more intuitive.
The best way to master REST APIs is by building real-world projects. Start with simple CRUD applications, experiment with different HTTP methods, and gradually incorporate features like authentication, validation, and error handling. Every project you build will strengthen your understanding and prepare you for real software development.
Thank you for taking the time to read this article. I hope it helped you understand REST APIs in a simple and practical way. If you found it useful, consider sharing it with fellow developers or leaving your thoughts in the comments.
Happy coding! 🚀
